campaign: F-WEDGE-SMALL-M-GEMV-WALL — hand-emit GEMV vs cuBLAS at small M
host: ubu-2 RTX 5070 sm_120 (driver 580, cuBLAS 12, FP32 peak 31.2 TFLOPS)
method: cuEvent 20 warmup + 200 timed median, 3 independent runs
kernel: gemv_warpreduce_kernel — 4 warps/CTA, 16 cols/CTA via float4 B-loads, K_CHUNK=128 shared-mem A-tile, __shfl_xor_sync butterfly reduce
finding: The small-M GEMV 'wedge' is BANDWIDTH-BOUND, not compute-bound. The 99.05% sub-roofline gap reported in the 2026-05-27 oracle was measured against the FP32 COMPUTE peak (31.2 TFLOPS), but at M=1 the operation is fundamentally memory-bandwidth-bound (1.0 byte/FLOP arithmetic intensity, far below the 31.2 TFLOPS / 700 GB/s = 44.6 FLOP/byte machine balance). cuBLAS at M=1 already sustains ~590 GB/s = ~84% of RTX 5070's ~700 GB/s peak bandwidth. A hand-emit GEMV kernel with shared-mem A-tile + float4 B-loads + warp-shuffle reduce reaches the same ~84% BW envelope and ties cuBLAS within timer jitter (0.948x; FALSIFIED at the 1.05x bar). At M>=8 the handemit kernel degrades sharply (CTA-level M-loop saturates compute resources without tile-level reuse), confirming the kernel is at its design ceiling.
ruled_out_axis: Small-M GEMV is NOT a >=1.5x hexa-vs-cuBLAS wedge. cuBLAS SGEMM is already near the BW roofline at M=1.
why_estimate_was_wrong: The 5-10x estimate in the 2026-05-27 ranked-wedge table was computed by reading the FP32-compute sub-roofline ratio (99.05%) as recoverable. It is not — that gap is the natural arithmetic-intensity ceiling of skinny GEMV, not slack in cuBLAS's implementation. The correct ceiling is BW-bound: 700 / 590 = 1.19x at most, well below the 1.5x publishability threshold.
implication_for_remaining_wedges: rank-2/3 top-K fusion (1.68-1.80x) — still a viable wedge; epilogue work moves real FLOPs off the LM-head bottleneck.
publishability: negative result publishable per project.tape @D paper_negative_ok — deterministically rules out the small-M GEMV axis from the hexa custom-kernel campaign.
preserves: feedback_closure_is_physical_limit (closure = BW physical limit, not compute peak)
